text.title: Regulated Incident Response Protocol Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Regulated Incident Response Protocol application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: GDPR 72h clock starts on detection (not confirmation). HIPAA 60d clock starts on discovery. PCI: per acquirer/brand. Document the moment detection occurred.
rule.rationale: Misreading the clock-start = late notification = penalties; defensive logging at detection is the safe practice.

rule.statement: Isolate affected systems (network, accounts) BEFORE diagnostic investigation. Preserve evidence in read-only mode.
rule.rationale: Investigation can taint evidence; isolation + read-only forensics is the legally defensible sequence.

rule.statement: First external call: client's compliance officer / DPO. NOT regulator, NOT vendors, NOT public. They drive who else gets notified + when.
rule.rationale: Wrong-order notifications create regulatory + legal complications; compliance officer is the single decision point.

rule.statement: Hold pre-approved comm templates for: internal status, client compliance, customer notification, regulator notice. Drafting under clock pressure is the failure mode.
rule.rationale: Real incidents leave no time for drafting; pre-approved templates remove minutes from the 72h clock.

rule.statement: Postmortem is reviewed by client counsel before distribution. Mass-distributed postmortems can become discovery evidence.
rule.rationale: Postmortem language under privilege protects honest analysis; uncounseled distribution can backfire legally.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
